events.text: The patient is a 58-year-old female named Margaret Osei-Bonsu. She is of West African and British descent, works as a high school science teacher, is a never-smoker, and has a longstanding history of dysplastic nevus syndrome with multiple prior excisions of atypical nevi. Family history is notable for a maternal uncle with cutaneous melanoma.

At age 58 years, the patient had a diagnosis of Stage IV (AJCC 8th edition, M1c) malignant melanoma, cutaneous, arising from the back. Primary site: skin of trunk (ICD-O-3 site code C44.5). Histology: malignant melanoma, NOS (ICD-O-3 morphology code 8720/3). TNM staging: T4b N3c M1c (elevated LDH). Summary stage: distant. Relevant biomarkers: BRAF V600E mutation positive by IHC and confirmed by molecular testing; PD-L1 expression 30% (TPS); LDH 2.4x upper limit of normal at diagnosis. Metastatic sites at diagnosis: multiple subcutaneous and cutaneous satellite nodules on the trunk and left flank, bilateral axillary lymphadenopathy (bulky left axilla measuring 4.8 cm), and multiple hepatic metastases (largest 3.2 cm in segment VI). No brain metastases on MRI at diagnosis. ECOG performance status 1. Confirmation: POSITIVE HISTOLOGY. Tumor size (primary): 18 mm (Breslow thickness 4.2 mm), ulcerated, Clark level V.

At age 58 years, the patient had a pathology result of type ANATOMIC PATHOLOGY from excision of a left axillary lymph node. Gross description: 4.8 cm firm lymph node with tan-brown cut surface and areas of central necrosis. Microscopic: sheets of epithelioid melanoma cells with abundant cytoplasm, prominent nucleoli, and frequent mitoses (>10/mm2). Melanin pigment present focally. Immunohistochemistry: S100 positive, Melan-A positive, SOX10 positive, HMB-45 positive, Ki-67 approximately 45%. BRAF V600E immunohistochemistry (VE1 antibody): strongly positive (3+). PD-L1 (22C3 pharmDx): TPS 30%, CPS 55. Diagnosis: metastatic malignant melanoma consistent with cutaneous primary.

At age 58 years, the patient had a CT scan of the chest, abdomen, and pelvis with contrast (staging), which showed cancer. Findings: multiple bilateral axillary lymph nodes, largest on the left measuring 4.8 x 3.5 cm with central necrosis; no mediastinal or hilar adenopathy; multiple hypodense hepatic lesions in segments IV, VI, and VII, the largest measuring 3.2 x 2.8 cm in segment VI; three subcutaneous nodules along the left flank measuring up to 1.8 cm; spleen and other abdominal organs unremarkable; no pulmonary nodules; no osseous lesions. Impression: widespread metastatic disease with hepatic, lymph node, and cutaneous/subcutaneous involvement. Metastatic sites: liver (multiple), lymph nodes (bilateral axillary, left bulky), subcutaneous (left flank).

At age 58 years, the patient had an MRI of the brain with and without gadolinium contrast (staging), which showed no cancer. Findings: no intracranial metastases, no leptomeningeal enhancement, no mass effect; incidental 4 mm pineal cyst, stable in appearance; normal brain parenchyma. Impression: no evidence of intracranial metastatic disease.

At age 58 years, the patient had next generation sequencing performed for cutaneous melanoma based on a specimen obtained from the left axillary lymph node metastasis (formalin-fixed paraffin-embedded tissue). A 500-gene comprehensive solid tumor panel was used. Clinically actionable alterations: BRAF p.V600E (VAF 48%, pathogenic, FDA-approved targeted therapy available), CDKN2A homozygous deletion (pathogenic, loss of tumor suppressor), NF1 p.Q1300* nonsense mutation (VAF 52%, pathogenic, loss of function, associated with RAS pathway activation). Co-mutations and additional variants: TP53 p.R248W (VAF 41%, pathogenic), PTEN p.L108R (VAF 22%, likely pathogenic), RAC1 p.P29S (VAF 18%, likely pathogenic), ARID2 p.E1242fs (VAF 35%, likely pathogenic), TERT promoter C228T mutation (VAF 55%, pathogenic), MAP2K1 amplification (copy number gain, 5 copies). Additional copy number alterations: CDKN2A/2B deletion (homozygous), MYC gain (3 copies), CCND1 gain. No NRAS mutation detected. No KIT mutation detected. No fusion events detected. No microsatellite instability (MSS). Tumor mutational burden: 42 mutations/Mb (high). PD-L1 by NGS correlative analysis consistent with immunohistochemistry result.

At age 58 years, the patient had a new patient oncology consultation note. The patient presented with a several-month history of a rapidly enlarging left axillary mass, weight loss of 8 lbs over 2 months, and intermittent right upper quadrant discomfort. Physical examination revealed a 5 cm firm, fixed left axillary mass; three palpable subcutaneous nodules along the left flank; and mild right upper quadrant tenderness on deep palpation without hepatomegaly. The remainder of the examination was unremarkable. Labs notable for LDH 412 U/L (ULN 171), AST 62 U/L, ALT 48 U/L, total bilirubin 0.9 mg/dL, alkaline phosphatase 145 U/L, albumin 3.8 g/dL. CBC within normal limits. ECOG PS 1. Molecular profiling confirmed BRAF V600E with co-occurring NF1 loss and high TMB (42 mut/Mb). Given the BRAF V600E mutation, bulky hepatic disease with elevated LDH, and need for rapid disease control, the decision was made to initiate dabrafenib/trametinib targeted therapy as first-line treatment. Brain MRI negative. Staging complete. Discussed risks, benefits, and alternatives. Patient agreed to initiate treatment. Cancer present. No response to therapy yet (treatment naive at time of note).

At age 58 years, the patient received palliative-intent dabrafenib 150 mg orally twice daily and trametinib 2 mg orally once daily (BRAF/MEK inhibitor combination targeted therapy) as first-line systemic therapy for BRAF V600E-mutant metastatic cutaneous melanoma. Treatment start date corresponds to approximately 3 weeks after initial staging was complete.

At age 58 years (approximately 3 weeks after starting dabrafenib/trametinib), the patient developed grade 1 pyrexia (temperature 38.2 degrees Celsius), grade 1 fatigue, and grade 1 nausea. These were managed with a brief dabrafenib dose interruption of 3 days followed by restart at full dose. No dose reduction required. Events resolved within 1 week of dose interruption.

At age 58 years (approximately 8 weeks after initiating dabrafenib/trametinib), the patient had a CT scan of the chest, abdomen, and pelvis with contrast (restaging), which showed cancer with significant response. Findings: left axillary lymph node decreased from 4.8 cm to 1.9 cm; hepatic lesions markedly decreased in size, largest now 1.1 cm (previously 3.2 cm); subcutaneous nodules on left flank decreased from 1.8 cm to 0.6 cm; no new lesions identified; LDH normalized to 155 U/L. Impression: partial response by RECIST 1.1 criteria with approximately 60% decrease in sum of target lesions. Marked radiographic improvement consistent with BRAF-targeted therapy response. Metastatic sites: liver (decreased, residual disease), lymph nodes (decreased, residual disease), subcutaneous (decreased, residual disease).

At age 58 years (8 weeks on dabrafenib/trametinib), the patient had an oncology follow-up visit. She reported significant clinical improvement with resolution of right upper quadrant discomfort, improvement in energy level, and weight regain of 5 lbs. Physical examination: left axillary mass now soft and approximately 2 cm, barely palpable subcutaneous nodules. Labs: LDH normalized (155 U/L), liver function tests within normal limits. Imaging showed impressive partial response. The patient tolerated therapy well after initial febrile episode. Plan to continue dabrafenib/trametinib at current doses with close monitoring. Cancer present. Significant response to therapy.

At age 58 years (approximately 5 months after starting dabrafenib/trametinib), the patient developed grade 2 palmar-plantar erythrodysesthesia (hand-foot skin reaction) affecting bilateral palms and soles, associated with grade 2 hyperkeratosis. Managed with intensive moisturizing cream, dose reduction of trametinib to 1.5 mg once daily, and urea 40% cream applied twice daily. Symptoms improved to grade 1 within 3 weeks, at which point trametinib was resumed at full dose.

At age 58 years (approximately 6 months after initiating dabrafenib/trametinib), the patient had a CT scan of the chest, abdomen, and pelvis with contrast (restaging), which showed cancer with stable disease following prior response. Findings: left axillary lymph node stable at 1.8 cm; hepatic lesions stable, largest 1.0 cm; subcutaneous nodules on left flank barely visible at 0.4 cm; no new lesions; LDH 160 U/L (within normal limits). Impression: continued partial response, stable compared to prior scan, no evidence of new disease or progression. Metastatic sites: liver (stable, residual), lymph nodes (stable, residual), subcutaneous (minimal, stable).

At age 58 years (approximately 8 months after initiating dabrafenib/trametinib), the patient had a CT scan of the chest, abdomen, and pelvis with contrast (restaging), which showed cancer with progression. Findings: left axillary lymph node increased from 1.8 cm to 3.4 cm with new central necrosis; hepatic lesions increased in size, largest now 2.6 cm (previously 1.0 cm) in segment VI, and two new hepatic lesions in segment II (1.5 cm) and segment IVa (1.2 cm); subcutaneous nodule on left flank re-enlarged to 1.4 cm; new small right inguinal lymph node 1.2 cm identified; LDH re-elevated at 285 U/L. Impression: progressive disease by RECIST 1.1 criteria. New hepatic lesions and enlargement of all prior target lesions consistent with acquired resistance to BRAF/MEK inhibition. Metastatic sites: liver (progressive, new lesions), lymph nodes (axillary progressive, new inguinal), subcutaneous (progressive).

At age 58 years (8 months on dabrafenib/trametinib), the patient had an oncology visit for restaging and management of disease progression. The patient reported new onset of left axillary pain, increasing fatigue (ECOG PS now 1-2), and recurrence of mild right upper quadrant discomfort. Physical examination revealed re-enlargement of left axillary mass to approximately 3.5 cm, with new right inguinal lymphadenopathy. Labs: LDH 285 U/L (elevated), liver enzymes mildly elevated (AST 55, ALT 44). CT confirmed progressive disease with new hepatic lesions and lymph node progression. Brain MRI ordered. Given progression on BRAF/MEK inhibition and prior high TMB and PD-L1 expression, transition to combination ipilimumab/nivolumab immunotherapy planned. Dabrafenib/trametinib to be discontinued. Patient counseled extensively regarding immune-related adverse events. Cancer present. Progression on prior therapy.

At age 58 years (at time of progression on dabrafenib/trametinib), the patient had an MRI of the brain with and without gadolinium contrast, which showed no cancer. Findings: no intracranial metastases identified; no leptomeningeal disease; no new findings compared to prior brain MRI performed at initial staging; incidental pineal cyst stable. Impression: no intracranial metastatic disease.

At age 58 years, the patient received palliative-intent ipilimumab 3 mg/kg intravenously every 3 weeks combined with nivolumab 1 mg/kg intravenously every 3 weeks (dual checkpoint inhibitor immunotherapy) as second-line systemic therapy for BRAF V600E-mutant metastatic cutaneous melanoma after progression on dabrafenib/trametinib. The combination was planned for 4 cycles (12 weeks), followed by nivolumab maintenance at 480 mg every 4 weeks.

At age 58 years (approximately 5 weeks after initiating ipilimumab/nivolumab, after the second cycle), the patient developed grade 3 immune-mediated colitis. She presented with 10-12 loose/watery bowel movements per day associated with crampy lower abdominal pain, hematochezia, and low-grade fever (38.1 degrees Celsius). Stool cultures and C. difficile toxin assay were negative. Flexible sigmoidoscopy showed diffuse mucosal edema, erythema, and focal ulcerations from rectum to the descending colon, consistent with immune-related colitis. Biopsies showed active colitis with increased intraepithelial lymphocytes. Both ipilimumab and nivolumab were held. The patient was hospitalized and started on methylprednisolone 1 mg/kg/day intravenously. No improvement after 3 days of IV steroids, so infliximab 5 mg/kg intravenously was administered (one dose). Symptoms dramatically improved within 48 hours of infliximab. The patient was transitioned to oral prednisone 1 mg/kg/day and discharged. Prednisone was tapered over 8 weeks. Gastroenterology was involved in co-management.

At age 58 years (following hospitalization for grade 3 immune-mediated colitis), the patient had an oncology follow-up visit after completing steroid taper. Bowel function returned to baseline (1-2 formed stools per day, no hematochezia). The patient had lost 4 lbs during the hospitalization but was recovering well. ECOG PS 1. Labs: CBC within normal limits, CRP normalized, liver function tests within normal limits, TSH 5.8 mIU/L (mildly elevated; repeat in 4 weeks). Given the severity of the colitis and the fact that only 2 cycles of ipilimumab/nivolumab were administered (partial induction), the ipilimumab was permanently discontinued. Plan to resume nivolumab monotherapy at 480 mg every 4 weeks (maintenance dosing) after colitis fully resolved and patient off steroids for at least 2 weeks. Cancer present. Response to therapy uncertain given toxicity interruption.

At age 58 years (approximately 3 months after immunotherapy initiation), the patient developed grade 1 hypothyroidism (TSH 8.4 mIU/L, free T4 0.8 ng/dL) consistent with immune-mediated thyroiditis. The patient reported mild fatigue and cold intolerance. Started on levothyroxine 50 mcg orally once daily with subsequent dose titration to 75 mcg once daily. TSH normalized on replacement therapy. This was managed as an outpatient and did not require immunotherapy interruption.

At age 58 years (approximately 10 weeks after the grade 3 colitis episode, once fully recovered and off steroids), the patient received nivolumab 480 mg intravenously every 4 weeks as single-agent maintenance immunotherapy. This represents the continuation of second-line immunotherapy following ipilimumab discontinuation due to toxicity.

At age 58 years (approximately 12 weeks after resuming nivolumab maintenance, approximately 5 months after first dose of ipilimumab/nivolumab), the patient had a CT scan of the chest, abdomen, and pelvis with contrast (restaging), which showed cancer with significant partial response. Findings: left axillary lymph node decreased from 3.4 cm to 1.3 cm; hepatic lesions substantially reduced, largest now 0.9 cm (previously 2.6 cm), segment II lesion 0.6 cm, segment IVa lesion no longer visible; subcutaneous nodule on left flank decreased to 0.5 cm; right inguinal lymph node decreased to 0.5 cm; no new lesions; LDH normalized to 148 U/L. Impression: partial response by RECIST 1.1 criteria, approximately 55% decrease in sum of target lesions since nadir on progression. Response attributable to ipilimumab/nivolumab followed by nivolumab maintenance. Metastatic sites: liver (residual, responding), lymph nodes (residual, responding), subcutaneous (minimal, responding).

At age 58 years (5 months after immunotherapy initiation, 3 months of nivolumab maintenance), the patient had an oncology follow-up visit. She reported marked clinical improvement, resolution of fatigue, and weight restoration to baseline. Bowel function remained normal. Thyroid function well-controlled on levothyroxine. Physical examination: left axillary lymph node barely palpable at approximately 1.5 cm; subcutaneous nodule very small and non-tender; no other lymphadenopathy. ECOG PS 0-1. Labs: LDH 148 U/L (normal), liver function tests normal, TSH 2.4 mIU/L on levothyroxine. Imaging confirmed excellent partial response to nivolumab maintenance. Plan to continue nivolumab 480 mg every 4 weeks with restaging every 3 months. Cancer present (residual). Significant response to therapy.

At age 59 years (approximately 9 months after immunotherapy initiation, 6 months of nivolumab maintenance), the patient had a CT scan of the chest, abdomen, and pelvis with contrast (restaging), which showed cancer with near-complete response. Findings: left axillary lymph node decreased to 0.8 cm (previously 1.3 cm); hepatic lesions: segment VI lesion now 0.4 cm, segment II lesion no longer measurable (<5 mm), segment IVa lesion resolved; subcutaneous nodule on left flank not identified; right inguinal lymph node 0.3 cm (not measurable); no new lesions; LDH 138 U/L (within normal limits). Impression: near-complete response by RECIST 1.1. Only minimal residual disease in left axilla and liver. Continued response on nivolumab maintenance. Metastatic sites: lymph nodes (minimal residual), liver (minimal residual).

At age 59 years (approximately 15 months after immunotherapy initiation, 12 months of nivolumab maintenance), the patient had a CT scan of the chest, abdomen, and pelvis with contrast, which showed no cancer by imaging criteria (complete radiographic response). Findings: left axillary lymph node now 0.6 cm (lymph node, likely reactive/fibrotic); no hepatic lesions identified; no subcutaneous nodules; no suspicious lymphadenopathy elsewhere; no new lesions; LDH 131 U/L (normal). Impression: complete radiographic response by RECIST 1.1 criteria. All previously identified target and non-target lesions resolved. No evidence of active metastatic disease on CT imaging. Continued nivolumab maintenance.

At age 59 years (at time of complete radiographic response on CT), the patient had an MRI of the brain with and without gadolinium contrast (surveillance), which showed no cancer. Findings: no intracranial metastases, no leptomeningeal disease, no new findings; pineal cyst stable. Impression: no intracranial metastatic disease.

At age 59 years (15 months after immunotherapy initiation, complete radiographic response on CT), the patient had an oncology follow-up visit. The patient reported excellent quality of life with ECOG PS 0. She had returned to full-time teaching. Bowel function normal, thyroid function well-controlled on levothyroxine 75 mcg daily. No new symptoms. Physical examination: no palpable lymphadenopathy, no palpable subcutaneous nodules, no hepatomegaly. Labs: LDH normal, liver function tests normal, CBC normal, TSH 2.1 mIU/L. CT chest/abdomen/pelvis showed complete radiographic response. Brain MRI negative. Discussion held regarding duration of nivolumab therapy. Given complete response after 12 months of nivolumab maintenance (approximately 15 months total on immunotherapy), plan discussed to consider stopping nivolumab after 2 years total of immunotherapy per emerging evidence. Patient understands and agrees to continue current schedule for now with reassessment. Cancer absent by imaging. Complete response to therapy.

At age 59 years (approximately 21 months after immunotherapy initiation, 18 months of nivolumab maintenance), the patient had a CT scan of the chest, abdomen, and pelvis with contrast (surveillance), which showed no cancer. Findings: no evidence of recurrent or metastatic disease; left axillary lymph node stable at 0.5 cm, consistent with fibrotic/reactive change; no hepatic lesions; no suspicious lymphadenopathy; no subcutaneous nodules; LDH 128 U/L. Impression: continued complete radiographic response. No evidence of active metastatic melanoma.

At age 59 years (21 months after immunotherapy initiation, sustained complete radiographic response), the patient had an oncology follow-up visit for surveillance and shared decision-making regarding nivolumab discontinuation. The patient remained in excellent health with ECOG PS 0. No immune-related adverse events since the initial colitis episode. Thyroid function stable on levothyroxine. Serial imaging showed sustained complete response for over 6 months. After extensive discussion of the risks and benefits of stopping versus continuing immunotherapy, including the emerging data suggesting that patients who achieve complete response on anti-PD-1 therapy can stop treatment, the patient and oncologist agreed to discontinue nivolumab after completing approximately 2 years total of checkpoint immunotherapy (including the 2 cycles of ipilimumab/nivolumab induction). Follow-up surveillance with CT imaging every 3-4 months for the first year after stopping, then every 6 months thereafter. Dermatology follow-up every 6 months for whole-body skin examination given history of dysplastic nevus syndrome. Cancer absent. Sustained complete response. Treatment discontinued electively.

documents.text:
ONCOLOGY FOLLOW-UP NOTE
Medical Oncology — Melanoma/Cutaneous Oncology Program

**REASON FOR VISIT:**
Scheduled 8-week on-treatment restaging visit and toxicity assessment. Patient is a 58-year-old female with BRAF V600E-mutant Stage IV (M1c) cutaneous melanoma of the trunk, currently on first-line dabrafenib (Tafinlar) 150 mg PO BID and trametinib (Mekinist) 2 mg PO daily. Today's visit includes review of restaging CT imaging, laboratory results, and treatment tolerability assessment.

---

**HISTORY OF PRESENT ILLNESS:**

Ms. Osei-Bonsu is a 58-year-old West African/British woman and high school science teacher who was diagnosed approximately 10 weeks ago with BRAF V600E-mutant Stage IV cutaneous melanoma arising from a primary on the trunk (Breslow thickness 4.2 mm, ulcerated, Clark level V). At diagnosis she had bulky left axillary nodal disease (4.8 cm), multiple hepatic metastases (largest 3.2 cm in segment VI), and subcutaneous/satellite nodules along the left flank. LDH was notably elevated at 2.4x the upper limit of normal (412 U/L). Comprehensive genomic profiling of the left axillary metastasis confirmed BRAF p.V600E (VAF 48%) as well as co-occurring NF1 loss-of-function mutation and high tumor mutational burden (42 mut/Mb). PD-L1 TPS was 30%. Brain MRI at diagnosis was negative.

Given the BRAF V600E mutation, the bulky hepatic disease, and the markedly elevated LDH indicating high tumor burden and need for rapid disease control, we initiated dabrafenib/trametinib combination targeted therapy as first-line treatment approximately 3 weeks after staging was complete. Shortly after starting therapy — at approximately the 3-week mark — she developed grade 1 pyrexia (Tmax 38.2°C), grade 1 fatigue, and grade 1 nausea. This classic dabrafenib-related febrile syndrome was managed with a 3-day dose interruption of dabrafenib (trametinib continued during the hold), followed by restart at full dose. Symptoms fully resolved within one week and no dose reduction was ultimately necessary.

She returns today for her 8-week on-treatment assessment. She reports a dramatic and subjectively meaningful clinical improvement since initiating therapy.

She describes complete resolution of the right upper quadrant heaviness/discomfort that had been bothersome at presentation, which she attributes to improvement in her hepatic disease. Her energy level has substantially improved — she describes returning to a nearly full activity level and has resumed light walking and some household activities she had curtailed prior to treatment. She has regained approximately 5 pounds, which she attributes to improved appetite and reduced nausea. She had lost 8 pounds in the 2 months prior to diagnosis. She is currently back at work part-time.

She denies any recurrent fever since the initial episode. She denies rash, photosensitivity, new skin lesions, or significant hand-foot skin changes. She denies visual disturbances, headache, or new neurological symptoms. She denies shortness of breath or cough. Bowel habits are normal with 1-2 formed stools daily. Denies hematochezia or mucus in stool. No dysuria. No new joint pain or swelling.

She is taking her medications as prescribed and has not missed doses since the restart after the brief interruption. She takes dabrafenib with plenty of water on an empty stomach (she confirmed she is dosing approximately 1 hour before meals), and takes trametinib at the same time each morning.

---

**REVIEW OF SYSTEMS:**

- Constitutional: Significant improvement in fatigue; weight gain of 5 lbs; no current fever, chills, or night sweats
- HEENT: No headache, no visual changes, no scleral icterus noted by patient
- Cardiovascular: No chest pain, no palpitations, no lower extremity swelling
- Pulmonary: No shortness of breath, no cough, no hemoptysis
- Gastrointestinal: Resolution of RUQ discomfort; good appetite; normal bowel function; no nausea currently, no vomiting, no diarrhea, no hematochezia
- Genitourinary: No dysuria, no hematuria
- Musculoskeletal: No new arthralgias or myalgias of significance
- Integumentary: No new suspicious moles or pigmented lesions reported by patient; no rash; no hand-foot skin changes; no photosensitivity reactions noted
- Neurological: No headache, no focal neurologic deficits, no cognitive changes
- Endocrine: No symptoms of adrenal insufficiency or thyroid dysfunction
- Psychiatric: Mood improved with clinical response; reports feeling "cautiously optimistic"

---

**PHYSICAL EXAMINATION:**

Vitals: Afebrile (temperature 36.8°C), blood pressure 118/72 mmHg, heart rate 74 bpm and regular, respiratory rate 16, SpO2 98% on room air, weight 64.3 kg (up from 59.8 kg at initial visit), height 165 cm, BMI 23.6 kg/m².

General: Well-appearing woman in no acute distress. Alert and oriented x3. Affect bright and engaged. Speaking comfortably. Appears noticeably healthier compared to initial consultation.

Skin: Multiple well-healed scars on back consistent with prior nevus excisions. Primary excision site on trunk with well-healed surgical scar, no evidence of local recurrence. Left flank subcutaneous nodules now barely appreciable — felt as three tiny, soft, non-tender, 4–6 mm bumps, markedly reduced from their prior 1.8 cm size. No new skin lesions or satellite nodules identified. No rash, no palmar erythema, no plantar changes, no photosensitivity eruption. Nails without periungual changes.

Head/Eyes/ENT: Atraumatic, normocephalic. Sclerae anicteric. No periorbital edema. Mucous membranes moist. Oropharynx clear.

Neck: Supple. No cervical or supraclavicular lymphadenopathy appreciated.

Cardiovascular: Regular rate and rhythm. Normal S1/S2. No murmurs, rubs, or gallops. No JVD. No peripheral edema.

Pulmonary: Clear to auscultation bilaterally. No wheezes, rales, or rhonchi.

Abdomen: Soft, non-distended, non-tender including in the right upper quadrant (notably improved from the mild tenderness at initial presentation). No hepatomegaly appreciated on deep palpation. Normoactive bowel sounds. No palpable splenomegaly. No ascites.

Axillary: Left axillary mass now soft, compressible, non-tender, and estimated at approximately 2 cm — dramatically reduced from the 5 cm firm, fixed, tender mass at initial presentation. Right axilla without significant lymphadenopathy. Right inguinal region without lymphadenopathy.

Musculoskeletal: No joint effusions or synovitis. Normal range of motion in major joints.

Neurological: Cranial nerves II–XII intact. Strength 5/5 in all extremities. Sensation grossly intact. Coordination and gait normal.

ECOG Performance Status: 1 (improved from ECOG 1-2 at presentation; borderline toward 0 today).

---

**LABORATORY RESULTS:**

Complete Blood Count:
- WBC 5.8 x10³/µL (within normal limits)
- Hemoglobin 12.4 g/dL (stable, mild normocytic anemia, likely chronic disease/prior disease burden, no change from prior)
- Platelets 218 x10³/µL
- Neutrophil count 3.4 x10³/µL
- Lymphocyte count 1.6 x10³/µL

Comprehensive Metabolic Panel:
- Sodium 139 mEq/L, Potassium 4.0 mEq/L, CO2 24 mEq/L, BUN 14 mg/dL, Creatinine 0.82 mg/dL (eGFR >60)
- Glucose 94 mg/dL
- Total bilirubin 0.7 mg/dL (previously 0.9 mg/dL)
- AST 28 U/L (previously 62 U/L — significant improvement)
- ALT 22 U/L (previously 48 U/L — significant improvement)
- Alkaline phosphatase 87 U/L (previously 145 U/L — significant improvement)
- Albumin 4.1 g/dL (previously 3.8 g/dL — improved)

LDH: 155 U/L (ULN 171 U/L) — NORMALIZED from 412 U/L at diagnosis. This represents a 62% reduction in LDH.

Thyroid function: TSH 2.2 mIU/L (within normal limits; no evidence of thyroid toxicity at this point).

Coagulation: PT/INR within normal limits.

Urinalysis: Unremarkable.

---

**IMAGING RESULTS:**

CT Chest/Abdomen/Pelvis with contrast (restaging, obtained within the past 5 days):

Left axilla: The bulky left axillary lymph node — the dominant mass that was 4.8 cm at baseline — has shrunk to 1.9 cm. The central necrosis previously visible is no longer appreciable. This is an extraordinary reduction of approximately 60% in the single largest node.

Liver: The hepatic disease has essentially melted away. The dominant segment VI lesion that measured 3.2 x 2.8 cm at baseline is now just 1.1 cm — a reduction of approximately 65%. The other hepatic lesions in segments IV and VII have reduced proportionally. No new hepatic lesions.

Left flank subcutaneous nodules: The most prominent subcutaneous satellite nodule measuring up to 1.8 cm at staging is now approximately 0.6 cm.

Chest: No pulmonary nodules or new lung lesions. No mediastinal or hilar adenopathy.

Other: No new lesions identified anywhere. No new lymphadenopathy. No bony lesions.

Laboratory correlate: LDH normalized to 155 U/L as noted above, which is consistent with the marked tumor regression seen on imaging.

Overall: The imaging reflects a striking partial response by any measure — the sum of target lesion measurements has decreased by approximately 60% from baseline, the disease is softer and less active on exam, and the metabolic marker (LDH) has normalized. The response is consistent with what is expected from dabrafenib/trametinib in BRAF V600E-mutant melanoma and appears to be occurring robustly.

Brain MRI: No brain imaging obtained today; baseline MRI at diagnosis was negative, and there are no symptoms or clinical indications to repeat at this time.

---

**ASSESSMENT:**

Ms. Osei-Bonsu is a 58-year-old woman with Stage IV BRAF V600E-mutant cutaneous melanoma who is currently on dabrafenib/trametinib (Tafinlar/Mekinist) for approximately 8 weeks as first-line therapy. She is demonstrating an excellent clinical and radiographic response to targeted therapy.

1. Metastatic melanoma, BRAF V600E-mutant, Stage IV M1c — currently responding to dabrafenib/trametinib.
   - Radiographic response is impressive and rapid, with ~60% reduction in tumor burden by imaging, LDH normalization, and dramatic clinical improvement including resolution of hepatic symptoms, weight regain, and improvement in performance status.
   - This response trajectory is typical for BRAF-targeted therapy and is very encouraging, though we always keep in mind that the median duration of response on BRAF/MEK inhibition is typically in the range of 9-12 months, and we will monitor closely for acquired resistance.
   - Will continue current therapy without dose modification given excellent tolerability.

2. Dabrafenib/trametinib tolerability — acceptable, no active toxicities.
   - The early grade 1 pyrexia episode resolved completely with a brief 3-day hold and did not recur after restart.
   - At this 8-week timepoint, no significant new toxicities are present. No rash, no hand-foot skin changes, no uveitis, no new skin lesions (including no squamous cell carcinomas or new keratoacanthomas, which are occasionally seen with BRAF inhibitor monotherapy but less commonly with the combination).
   - LFTs and CBC are all within normal limits, improved from baseline.
   - Counseled patient again on signs and symptoms to monitor for, including fever (dabrafenib-related pyrexia can recur and requires dose interruption), any new skin lesions (warrant dermatology review), visual changes (ophthalmology evaluation if they occur), and GI symptoms.

3. Performance status — ECOG 1, trending toward 0. Improved significantly from baseline.

---

**PLAN:**

1. Continue dabrafenib 150 mg PO BID and trametinib 2 mg PO daily at current doses. Reinforce dosing instructions — dabrafenib on empty stomach (no food within 1 hour), trametinib once daily at the same time.

2. Next restaging CT chest/abdomen/pelvis with contrast in approximately 8 weeks (cumulative ~6 months on therapy). Will obtain LDH and liver function tests at that time as well.

3. No brain MRI indicated today. Will obtain if any neurological symptoms develop or if a future timepoint warrants it.

4. Dermatology appointment scheduled in 4 weeks for whole-body skin examination, given her baseline history of dysplastic nevus syndrome. She has been instructed to conduct monthly self-skin examinations and to report any new or changing skin lesions promptly.

5. Advised patient to call immediately for fever >38.0°C (and to hold dabrafenib and come in or call within 24 hours), any significant nausea/vomiting, visual changes, new painful or rapidly enlarging skin lesions, or any concerning new symptoms.

6. Supportive care: Encouraged continued oral hydration, good nutrition, and gradual increase in activity as tolerated.

7. Psychosocial: Patient is coping well with good social support. Will continue to monitor; social work referral available if needed.

8. Follow-up: Return in approximately 8 weeks for restaging, or sooner if clinical concerns arise.

Total visit time: 45 minutes. Greater than 50% of visit time spent in counseling and coordination of care.

2. Clinical correlation and reassessment of the current treatment strategy is warranted.

HISTORY OF PRESENT ILLNESS

Ms. Osei-Bonsu is a 58-year-old woman with a history of dysplastic nevus syndrome who was diagnosed with BRAF V600E-mutant Stage IV cutaneous melanoma arising from the trunk approximately 9–10 months ago. Her disease at diagnosis was notable for bulky left axillary lymphadenopathy (4.8 cm with central necrosis), multiple hepatic metastases (largest 3.2 cm in segment VI), and subcutaneous satellite nodules along the left flank. She had an elevated LDH of 412 U/L at diagnosis. Comprehensive molecular profiling by a 500-gene solid tumor NGS panel revealed BRAF p.V600E (VAF 48%), co-occurring NF1 p.Q1300* loss-of-function mutation, CDKN2A homozygous deletion, TERT promoter C228T, and TP53 p.R248W, with a high tumor mutational burden of 42 mutations/Mb and PD-L1 TPS 30%. Brain MRI at staging was negative for intracranial disease.

Given the BRAF V600E mutation, bulky hepatic disease, significantly elevated LDH, and clinical urgency for disease control, she was initiated on first-line dabrafenib/trametinib approximately 3 weeks after completing staging. Her early course was complicated by grade 1 pyrexia (Tmax 38.2°C), fatigue, and nausea approximately 3 weeks into treatment, managed with a brief 3-day dabrafenib hold followed by restart at full dose without formal dose reduction — toxicities resolved within one week.

At her 8-week restaging CT, she demonstrated a dramatic response: the left axillary node shrank from 4.8 cm to 1.9 cm, the dominant hepatic lesion decreased from 3.2 cm to 1.1 cm, and subcutaneous nodules regressed from 1.8 cm to 0.6 cm. LDH had normalized to 155 U/L. She reported feeling substantially better, with resolution of RUQ discomfort and recovery of lost weight. Treatment was continued at full doses.

At approximately 5 months into therapy, she developed grade 2 palmar-plantar erythrodysesthesia (PPE) affecting bilateral palms and soles with associated hyperkeratosis. Trametinib was dose-reduced to 1.5 mg QD with intensive urea cream and emollient use. Symptoms improved to grade 1 within 3 weeks and trametinib was successfully escalated back to 2 mg QD.

At the 6-month restaging CT, disease remained stable and in partial response (left axillary node 1.8 cm, dominant hepatic lesion 1.0 cm, subcutaneous nodule 0.4 cm), with LDH continuing within normal limits at 160 U/L.

The patient now returns approximately 8 months after treatment initiation with the above new symptoms. Today's restaging CT (reviewed in preparation for this visit) demonstrates unequivocal disease progression: the left axillary node has enlarged from 1.8 cm to 3.4 cm with new central necrosis, the dominant hepatic segment VI lesion has grown from 1.0 cm to 2.6 cm, two new hepatic lesions are present in segments II (1.5 cm) and IVa (1.2 cm), the left flank subcutaneous nodule has re-enlarged to 1.4 cm, and there is a new right inguinal lymph node measuring 1.2 cm — a site not previously involved. LDH has re-elevated to 285 U/L. Brain MRI has been ordered and is pending at the time of this visit (results to be reviewed upon availability).

These findings are entirely consistent with acquired resistance to BRAF/MEK-targeted therapy, which is well-recognized after a median of approximately 6–12 months on BRAF/MEK inhibitor combinations. Given the genomic context — particularly co-occurring NF1 loss-of-function, MAP2K1 amplification, and high TMB — multiple potential resistance mechanisms may be operative, including RAS-pathway reactivation and MEK bypass signaling. The clinical picture is one of classic acquired resistance.

The patient has been counseled at length today regarding the findings and the rationale for transitioning therapy.

ASSESSMENT

Ms. Osei-Bonsu is a 58-year-old woman with BRAF V600E-mutant, NF1-comutated Stage IV metastatic cutaneous melanoma who initially responded very well to first-line dabrafenib/trametinib — achieving a near-50–60% reduction in disease burden as early as 8 weeks into therapy, with LDH normalization and significant symptomatic improvement. She now presents at approximately 8 months with clear radiographic and clinical evidence of acquired resistance to BRAF/MEK inhibition. This is expected given that the median duration of response to this class of agents in metastatic melanoma is typically in the range of 9–12 months, and her molecular profile (NF1 loss, MAP2K1 amplification, high mutational burden) suggests a biologically complex tumor with multiple potential bypass mechanisms.

The re-elevation of LDH to 285 U/L (approximately 1.7x ULN), the return of hepatic and nodal disease, the appearance of two new hepatic lesions, and the involvement of a new inguinal nodal station collectively indicate that the disease is actively progressing and that continued dabrafenib/trametinib will not provide meaningful benefit. There are no features of pseudoprogression in this context — targeted therapy does not produce delayed responses.

She has a highly actionable immunologic profile: PD-L1 TPS 30%, and a very high TMB of 42 mutations/Mb. She did not receive upfront immunotherapy (a reasonable decision given the urgent visceral disease control need at diagnosis). Her performance status remains ECOG 1–2, she has no active autoimmune disease, no prior immune-related adverse events, and is otherwise a good candidate for dual checkpoint blockade with ipilimumab and nivolumab. This combination (ipi 3 mg/kg + nivo 1 mg/kg every 3 weeks x 4 cycles, then nivo maintenance) represents the standard second-line approach in this setting and offers the potential for durable responses.

Key concerns prior to initiating checkpoint immunotherapy:
1. Brain MRI result — must confirm absence of symptomatic or large brain metastases before proceeding with ipi/nivo; if small asymptomatic brain metastases are identified, combination immunotherapy remains an option but would merit neurosurgery or radiation oncology co-evaluation.
2. Liver function — current mild transaminase and alkaline phosphatase elevations are most likely attributable to progressive hepatic metastases rather than drug-induced liver injury (as she is being transitioned off dabrafenib/trametinib). These should be monitored closely, as hepatic immune-related adverse events can complicate ipi/nivo therapy.
3. Bowel history — she has no prior history of inflammatory bowel disease. She should be counseled that immune-mediated colitis is the most common serious adverse event with ipi/nivo, occurring in 15–25% of patients, and that prompt reporting of diarrhea is imperative.

3. Toxicity Counseling — Immune-Related Adverse Events (irAEs):
   - Extensive patient education performed today regarding the spectrum of irAEs associated with ipi/nivo, with particular emphasis on:
     a. Colitis/diarrhea: Instruct to call if > 4 bowel movements above baseline per day, or any bloody/mucous stools — do NOT wait for next scheduled visit.
     b. Hepatitis: Liver function will be checked before each cycle.
     c. Pneumonitis: New or worsening cough, dyspnea, or O2 requirement — immediate evaluation.
     d. Endocrinopathies: Fatigue, headache, visual changes (pituitary), polyuria/polydipsia (diabetes insipidus or type 1 DM), new cold intolerance (thyroid).
     e. Dermatitis: Rash, pruritus — report grade 2 or higher.
     f. Neurotoxicity: Any new focal neurological symptoms.
   - Written irAE management card provided. 24-hour oncology phone number reviewed.
   - Patient expressed understanding and appropriate concern; verbalized willingness to call with any symptoms.

4. Labs (today):
   - CBC, CMP, LFTs, LDH, CRP, TSH reviewed above. Mild anemia and LFT elevation noted — attributed to disease progression. Will repeat in 2 weeks to ensure no ongoing deterioration prior to cycle 1.

5. Hepatic Lesions and LFT Monitoring:
   - Given presence of hepatic metastases and mildly elevated LFTs, a hepatology consult is not indicated at this time, but LFTs will be closely monitored pre-cycle and mid-cycle.
   - If LFTs worsen substantially before immunotherapy initiation, reassessment and potential hepatology involvement will occur.

6. Supportive Care:
   - Acetaminophen PRN for axillary pain (avoid NSAIDs given hepatic involvement and potential immunotherapy interactions).
   - Nutritional counseling referral placed given mild decrease in appetite and anemia.
   - Social work referral renewed — patient is on reduced-schedule work status and may need additional support during a new and more intense treatment phase.

7. Dermatology Follow-up:
   - Recommend dermatology follow-up for whole-body skin examination given dysplastic nevus syndrome history; any new or changing lesions on the back/trunk require prompt evaluation.

8. Next Oncology Appointment:
   - Will be seen in clinic on day 1 of cycle 1 of ipilimumab/nivolumab (approximately 1–2 weeks from today).
   - Subsequent visits will be every 3 weeks for the duration of induction (cycles 1–4), then every 4 weeks on nivolumab maintenance.
   - Restaging CT chest/abdomen/pelvis planned after 4 cycles of induction (approximately 12 weeks from first immunotherapy dose).

9. Brain MRI Results Follow-Up:
   - Nurse coordinator to contact patient within 48 hours with MRI result.
   - If negative: confirm cycle 1 scheduling.
   - If positive: urgent multidisciplinary discussion.

Time spent with patient: approximately 55 minutes, including detailed discussion of imaging findings, change of therapy rationale, and irAE counseling. All questions answered. Patient verbalized understanding and consent for the proposed treatment transition.

Post-Hospitalization Visit — Immune-Mediated Colitis Recovery / Immunotherapy Resumption Planning

CHIEF COMPLAINT
Margaret Osei-Bonsu is a 58-year-old female with Stage IV BRAF V600E-mutant metastatic cutaneous melanoma who presents for oncology follow-up approximately 2 weeks following hospital discharge after a grade 3 immune-mediated colitis requiring IV methylprednisolone and infliximab. She is here to assess symptom resolution, complete steroid taper, and discuss the plan for resuming immunotherapy.

---

HISTORY OF PRESENT ILLNESS
Ms. Osei-Bonsu's oncologic history is well known to this service. She was diagnosed with BRAF V600E-mutant Stage IV metastatic cutaneous melanoma approximately 14 months ago with bulky left axillary adenopathy (4.8 cm), multiple hepatic metastases (largest 3.2 cm), and subcutaneous satellite nodules on the left flank. LDH was markedly elevated at 2.4x ULN at diagnosis. She completed comprehensive molecular profiling which confirmed BRAF p.V600E (VAF 48%), co-occurring NF1 p.Q1300* loss-of-function mutation, high TMB (42 mutations/Mb), and PD-L1 TPS 30% / CPS 55. Brain MRI at diagnosis was negative.

She was initiated on dabrafenib 150 mg twice daily plus trametinib 2 mg once daily as first-line therapy and had an excellent initial response — LDH normalized and imaging at 8 weeks showed approximately 60% reduction in tumor burden. She experienced a brief febrile episode at approximately 3 weeks (grade 1 pyrexia requiring 3-day dabrafenib hold) and later developed grade 2 palmar-plantar erythrodysesthesia at approximately 5 months (trametinib dose reduced to 1.5 mg, symptoms resolved to grade 1, trametinib re-escalated). Disease remained well-controlled through the 6-month scan.

Unfortunately, at approximately 8 months on dabrafenib/trametinib, restaging CT demonstrated clear progression — re-enlargement of the left axillary mass to 3.4 cm with necrosis, growth of hepatic lesions with two new hepatic lesions, re-enlargement of the left flank subcutaneous nodule, and a new right inguinal lymph node. LDH rose to 285 U/L. Brain MRI at the time of progression was again negative. In light of the progression on BRAF/MEK inhibition, the patient's high TMB, and meaningful PD-L1 expression, she was transitioned to second-line combination checkpoint immunotherapy: ipilimumab 3 mg/kg IV every 3 weeks plus nivolumab 1 mg/kg IV every 3 weeks, planned for 4 cycles of induction followed by nivolumab 480 mg every 4 weeks as maintenance.

She received Cycle 1 of ipi/nivo without acute infusion reactions and was given Cycle 2 on schedule. Approximately 5 weeks after starting (roughly 2 weeks after Cycle 2), she developed 10-12 watery/loose bowel movements per day with crampy lower abdominal pain, hematochezia, and low-grade fever. Stool cultures and C. diff were negative. Flexible sigmoidoscopy demonstrated diffuse mucosal erythema, edema, and focal ulcerations from the rectum to the descending colon; biopsies confirmed active colitis with increased intraepithelial lymphocytes. She was admitted and started on methylprednisolone 1 mg/kg/day IV. After 3 days without adequate improvement, infliximab 5 mg/kg IV was administered as a single dose. Bowel symptoms responded dramatically within 48 hours of infliximab. Both ipilimumab and nivolumab were held. She was transitioned to oral prednisone 1 mg/kg/day and discharged in stable condition. GI was involved throughout her hospitalization.

Today she returns for post-discharge follow-up. She reports that her bowel function has fully returned to baseline — she is having 1 to 2 well-formed stools per day with no blood, no urgency, and no cramping. She has completed approximately 8 weeks of steroid taper per schedule, having tapered from prednisone 1 mg/kg down to 10 mg daily over the preceding 4 weeks, with a plan to discontinue steroids completely over the next 1-2 weeks as tolerated. She has been off steroids for approximately 10 days at this visit. She notes mild fatigue that has been slowly improving, and she endorses mild cold intolerance over the past few weeks — she has been wearing an extra layer at home. No new rashes. No visual changes. No arthralgias or myalgias. No headache or new neurological symptoms. She lost approximately 4 lbs during the hospitalization but reports she has been eating normally since discharge. Appetite is back. No nausea, vomiting, or dysphagia.

She has a scheduled return to work (high school science teacher) planned for next month. She reports her mood is "cautiously optimistic" and is asking when she can restart her immunotherapy.

---

---

ASSESSMENT AND PLAN

1. Stage IV BRAF V600E-mutant metastatic cutaneous melanoma — status post progression on dabrafenib/trametinib, 2 cycles of ipilimumab/nivolumab completed (held for grade 3 immune colitis). Disease status by imaging currently uncertain given interval since last scan; clinical exam mildly reassuring (no dramatic new masses) but not definitive.

   - Given the severity of the grade 3 colitis, ipilimumab is permanently discontinued. The patient received only 2 of the planned 4 induction cycles; however, restarting ipilimumab in the setting of prior grade 3 immune colitis requiring infliximab is not appropriate per current guidelines and institutional practice.
   - Plan to resume nivolumab (Opdivo) monotherapy at 480 mg IV every 4 weeks (maintenance dosing) once the following criteria are met: (1) colitis fully resolved (bowel function at baseline — met today), (2) patient off systemic corticosteroids for at least 2 weeks (anticipated within the next 10–14 days with the final prednisone taper), and (3) no other active immune-related adverse events. Based on today's assessment, if the final prednisone taper proceeds as planned, the patient should be eligible to resume nivolumab in approximately 3 to 4 weeks.
   - Patient was counseled that single-agent nivolumab, while representing a reduction in the intensity of the checkpoint inhibitor combination, still has meaningful activity in BRAF-mutant melanoma, and given this patient's very high TMB (42 mut/Mb) and significant PD-L1 expression (CPS 55), there is a reasonable basis for expecting benefit from anti-PD-1 monotherapy even after the abbreviated ipi/nivo induction.
   - Restaging CT chest/abdomen/pelvis to be scheduled approximately 8–12 weeks after resuming nivolumab. We will not scan at this time given the patient is completing a steroid course and clinically appears stable.
   - Patient was counseled to watch closely for any signs of colitis recurrence once nivolumab is restarted (increased stool frequency, blood in stool, cramping) and to contact the oncology nurse triage line immediately if these symptoms develop. GI is aware of the plan to rechallenge with single-agent anti-PD-1 and is available for consultation.

2. Grade 3 immune-mediated colitis — resolving. Bowel function at baseline today. Completing final prednisone taper (currently at 5 mg daily, plan to discontinue in 1 week). Patient instructed to take the taper as follows: 5 mg daily x7 days, then stop, with follow-up call in 1 week. CRP normalizing. Patient to avoid NSAIDs. Continue high-fiber, bowel-friendly diet. Gastroenterology follow-up appointment confirmed in 6 weeks. Will check stool calprotectin at next visit.

3. Thyroid function — subclinical hypothyroidism, likely immune-mediated (immune checkpoint inhibitor-associated thyroiditis). TSH 5.8 mIU/L with low-normal free T4, in the setting of mild cold intolerance and fatigue. This is consistent with evolving immunotherapy-associated thyroiditis, which is a well-recognized adverse effect of anti-PD-1 therapy. The patient was informed of this finding.
   - Will recheck TSH and free T4 in 4 weeks (approximately at the time of the next visit).
   - If TSH remains elevated or rises further, or if free T4 declines below the normal range, will initiate levothyroxine replacement. At this point, given that TSH is only mildly elevated (5.8) and free T4 remains within normal limits (though at the low-normal end), a watchful waiting approach is reasonable for the next 4 weeks. Patient was counseled to report worsening fatigue, significant cold intolerance, constipation, dry skin, hair loss, or any other hypothyroid symptoms.
   - Endocrinology referral is not required at this point; will manage thyroid issues in the oncology setting and consult endocrinology if the situation becomes more complex.

4. Mild anemia — hemoglobin 11.8 g/dL, likely multifactorial (recent acute illness, nutritional depletion, possible mild anemia of chronic disease). Iron studies and reticulocyte count ordered. Will recheck CBC at next visit. No symptoms of severe anemia; no transfusion indicated at this time. Encourage iron-rich diet and nutritional recovery.

5. Nutrition and weight — the patient lost approximately 4 lbs during the acute colitis episode. Appetite has returned to normal. Encouraged to continue working on nutritional recovery. Dietitian referral placed.

6. Dysplastic nevus syndrome — ongoing dermatology surveillance. Dermatology appointment confirmed; patient reminded to perform monthly self-skin exams and to report any changing lesions.

7. Psychosocial — patient reports mild anxiety related to uncertainty about disease status and immunotherapy resumption. She was provided with reassurance and thorough explanation of the next steps. Social work referral offered; patient declined at this time, stating her family support system is strong. She expressed eagerness to return to work and was encouraged.

Return to clinic in 4 weeks (or sooner if concerns arise), at which time we anticipate the patient will have completed steroid taper, will be eligible to resume nivolumab, and we will recheck thyroid function. Orders placed for TSH/free T4 in 4 weeks, CBC in 4 weeks, iron studies today, and restaging CT approximately 8 weeks after nivolumab resumption.

HISTORY OF PRESENT ILLNESS

Ms. Osei-Bonsu is a 58-year-old female with a history of dysplastic nevus syndrome who was diagnosed with stage IV cutaneous melanoma arising from the back approximately 5 months ago, with metastatic involvement of the liver, bilateral axillary lymph nodes, and subcutaneous tissues of the left flank at presentation. Her primary lesion demonstrated a Breslow thickness of 4.2 mm with ulceration (T4b), and molecular testing confirmed a BRAF V600E mutation. Next-generation sequencing additionally demonstrated co-occurring NF1 loss-of-function mutation, CDKN2A homozygous deletion, and a high tumor mutational burden of 42 mutations/Mb. PD-L1 TPS was 30% by 22C3 pharmDx at diagnosis. LDH was significantly elevated at 2.4x the upper limit of normal at presentation.

Given the presence of the BRAF V600E mutation, bulky hepatic disease, and the need for rapid disease control in the setting of elevated LDH and M1c staging, she was initiated on first-line targeted therapy with dabrafenib 150 mg twice daily and trametinib 2 mg once daily. She achieved a marked radiographic response by 8 weeks -- left axillary lymph node had decreased from 4.8 cm to approximately 1.9 cm, and hepatic metastases had shrunk dramatically, with the largest lesion going from 3.2 cm to 1.1 cm. She experienced a transient low-grade fever and nausea in the first weeks of targeted therapy requiring a brief (3-day) dabrafenib hold, with full resolution. At approximately 5 months on therapy, she developed grade 2 palmar-plantar erythrodysesthesia requiring a temporary trametinib dose reduction to 1.5 mg, with subsequent full-dose resumption.

Unfortunately, restaging at approximately 8 months on dabrafenib/trametinib revealed acquired resistance with disease progression: the left axillary lymph node had grown from 1.8 cm back to 3.4 cm, hepatic lesions had re-enlarged (largest from 1.0 cm to 2.6 cm) with two new hepatic lesions (segment II, 1.5 cm; segment IVa, 1.2 cm), left flank subcutaneous nodule had re-enlarged to 1.4 cm, and a new right inguinal lymph node measuring 1.2 cm had appeared. LDH re-elevated to 285 U/L. Brain MRI performed at the time of progression again showed no intracranial metastases. Dabrafenib/trametinib was discontinued and she was transitioned to second-line immunotherapy with the ipilimumab/nivolumab combination (ipi 3 mg/kg + nivo 1 mg/kg IV every 3 weeks), planned for 4 induction cycles followed by nivolumab maintenance at 480 mg every 4 weeks.

After completing 2 cycles of ipilimumab/nivolumab (approximately 5 weeks in), she was hospitalized for grade 3 immune-mediated colitis presenting with 10-12 watery bowel movements daily, crampy abdominal pain, and hematochezia. Sigmoidoscopy confirmed diffuse immune-related colitis. She required IV methylprednisolone and, given lack of improvement at 72 hours, received a single dose of infliximab 5 mg/kg with dramatic symptomatic improvement. Ipilimumab was permanently discontinued given the severity of the toxicity. She completed a prolonged 8-week prednisone taper as an outpatient.

Approximately 3 months after the initial ipilimumab/nivolumab induction, she was also noted to have developed grade 1 immune-mediated hypothyroidism (TSH 8.4 mIU/L, free T4 0.8 ng/dL), consistent with immune-related thyroiditis, and was started on levothyroxine, dose-titrated to 75 mcg daily with TSH normalization.

Once she had fully recovered from the colitis, was off corticosteroids for at least 2 weeks, and had stable thyroid function on hormone replacement, nivolumab maintenance was resumed at 480 mg IV every 4 weeks (approximately 10 weeks after the colitis hospitalization). Today's visit marks approximately 3 months of nivolumab maintenance (approximately 5 months total since the first dose of combination ipilimumab/nivolumab).

Since resuming nivolumab, the patient has been doing extremely well. She reports marked improvement in her energy level and overall functional status -- she has been able to return to her regular activities including full-time work as a high school science teacher. She denies any recurrence of gastrointestinal symptoms and has had no further episodes of diarrhea or abdominal cramping. She notes mild, intermittent fatigue at the end of the day but attributes this to a busy work schedule rather than any medication side effect. She has had no new skin changes, rashes, or joint pains. She denies any dyspnea, cough, or chest discomfort that would raise concern for immune-related pneumonitis. She denies headache, visual changes, or neurological symptoms. She reports good appetite with weight now restored to her pre-illness baseline. She reports cold intolerance has resolved since optimizing levothyroxine dose.

Restaging CT of the chest, abdomen, and pelvis with contrast performed in preparation for today's visit showed substantial improvement in all areas of previously known disease, to be discussed in detail below.

1. Stage IV (M1c) BRAF V600E-mutant cutaneous melanoma with continued excellent response to nivolumab maintenance immunotherapy.

Ms. Osei-Bonsu continues on the nivolumab maintenance phase of her second-line immunotherapy and is doing exceptionally well both clinically and radiographically. The clinical response achieved here is notable given the complexity of this patient's treatment history. She received a meaningful but truncated course of ipilimumab/nivolumab (only 2 of 4 planned induction cycles) due to grade 3 colitis. Despite this abbreviated induction, she has gone on to mount a robust and deepening immune response on nivolumab monotherapy maintenance. This is consistent with published data showing that even partial induction with the combination can prime durable responses, particularly in patients with favorable immunologic profiles such as high TMB and PD-L1 expression. Her TMB of 42 mutations/Mb is in the high range for melanoma and likely contributed to immunotherapy sensitivity. Her PD-L1 expression of 30% TPS (CPS 55) is also favorable.

CHIEF COMPLAINT:
Ms. Margaret Osei-Bonsu is a 59-year-old female with a history of BRAF V600E-mutant Stage IV cutaneous melanoma who presents for routine surveillance follow-up and discussion of nivolumab discontinuation. She is now approximately 21 months from her first dose of checkpoint immunotherapy and approximately 18 months into single-agent nivolumab maintenance. She has had a sustained complete response on CT imaging for the past two surveillance scans.

HISTORY OF PRESENT ILLNESS:
Ms. Osei-Bonsu is a delightful 59-year-old high school science teacher with a background history of dysplastic nevus syndrome who originally presented roughly a year and a half prior with a rapidly enlarging left axillary mass, weight loss, and right upper quadrant discomfort. She was diagnosed with Stage IV (M1c) cutaneous melanoma arising from the back with metastases to bilateral axillary lymph nodes, multiple hepatic segments, and subcutaneous nodules of the left flank. Molecular profiling revealed a BRAF V600E mutation, co-occurring NF1 loss, and a high tumor mutational burden of 42 mutations/Mb. PD-L1 TPS was 30%. LDH was markedly elevated at diagnosis (2.4x ULN).

Given bulky hepatic disease and an elevated LDH portending a poor prognosis, she was started on first-line dabrafenib 150 mg BID and trametinib 2 mg daily. She achieved a rapid, impressive partial response -- over 60% reduction in disease burden -- but unfortunately developed acquired resistance and radiographic progression at approximately 8 months. At that time, brain MRI was negative for intracranial disease.

She was transitioned to second-line ipilimumab (3 mg/kg) plus nivolumab (1 mg/kg) every 3 weeks. After cycle 2, she developed grade 3 immune-mediated colitis requiring hospitalization, IV methylprednisolone, and ultimately infliximab, after which ipilimumab was permanently discontinued. She recovered fully over the ensuing 8 weeks on a prolonged prednisone taper with gastroenterology co-management. A separate, milder immune-related event occurred in the form of hypothyroidism (TSH 8.4 mIU/L, free T4 low), for which she was started on levothyroxine, now titrated and stable at 75 mcg daily.

Following recovery from colitis, she resumed nivolumab 480 mg IV every 4 weeks as maintenance. Response to immunotherapy has been outstanding: the first restaging CT after resuming nivolumab showed a substantial partial response, followed by near-complete response at approximately 9 months, and a complete radiographic response confirmed on CT chest/abdomen/pelvis at the 15-month mark. The most recent CT approximately 3 months ago (approximately 21 months after immunotherapy initiation, 18 months of nivo maintenance) again demonstrated no evidence of active metastatic disease -- left axillary lymph node stable at 5 mm consistent with fibrotic/reactive change, no hepatic lesions, no suspicious adenopathy, LDH 128 U/L.

Brain MRI performed at the time of CT-documented complete response was also negative for intracranial disease.

She presents today for routine oncology follow-up and to continue the conversation regarding duration of therapy. ASSESSMENT AND PLAN:

Ms. Osei-Bonsu is a 59-year-old woman with previously aggressive Stage IV (M1c) BRAF V600E-mutant cutaneous melanoma who has achieved and maintained a remarkable complete response to immunotherapy. She is now 21 months from her first dose of ipilimumab/nivolumab and 18 months into single-agent nivolumab 480 mg maintenance. She has had a sustained complete response on serial imaging for over 6 months, LDH is normal, and she is clinically doing exceptionally well at ECOG PS 0 with no evidence of active disease.

1. METASTATIC MELANOMA -- COMPLETE RESPONSE, TREATMENT DISCONTINUATION:
After extensive shared decision-making discussion today, Ms. Osei-Bonsu and I have agreed to electively discontinue nivolumab. The rationale includes: (1) she has achieved and maintained a complete radiographic response for more than 6 consecutive months across two surveillance scans; (2) she is now approaching approximately 2 years of total checkpoint immunotherapy exposure, inclusive of the 2 induction cycles of ipilimumab/nivolumab; and (3) the evolving literature -- including data from the CheckMate and KEYNOTE trials as well as melanoma-specific experience -- suggests that patients who attain a durable complete response on anti-PD-1 therapy can often safely stop treatment with preserved benefit. We discussed that this is not a curative decision, that recurrence remains a real possibility, and that she may be considered for re-treatment if relapse occurs. She understood all of this and expressed a strong preference to stop, citing fatigue from monthly infusion visits and a desire to move forward with her life. This decision is entirely reasonable and supported. Today's planned nivolumab infusion will be the final one. She will not receive additional checkpoint immunotherapy unless disease recurrence is documented.

2. SURVEILLANCE PLAN POST-TREATMENT DISCONTINUATION:
Given the high-risk nature of her prior disease (stage M1c, elevated LDH at diagnosis, bulky hepatic involvement) and the reality that late relapses can occur even after complete response, close surveillance is warranted. Plan:
- CT chest/abdomen/pelvis with contrast every 3 months for the first year off therapy.
- Brain MRI every 6 months for the first year, then annually if stable.
- LDH and LFTs at each clinic visit.
- Transition to CT every 6 months in year 2 if no recurrence, with ongoing clinical judgment thereafter.

3. IMMUNE-RELATED HYPOTHYROIDISM:
Well-controlled on levothyroxine 75 mcg daily. TSH 1.9 mIU/L today, which is excellent. Will continue current dose. Recheck TSH in 3 months at next surveillance visit. She remains aware that thyroid function should continue to be monitored even after immunotherapy is stopped, as thyroid dysfunction can persist.

4. DYSPLASTIC NEVUS SYNDROME / DERMATOLOGIC SURVEILLANCE:
To be seen in dermatology every 6 months for whole-body skin examination. Last dermatology visit was approximately 2 months ago with no concerning findings. Next scheduled in approximately 4 months. This is important given her personal history of dysplastic nevi and family history of cutaneous melanoma.

5. PSYCHOSOCIAL SUPPORT AND PATIENT EDUCATION:
We discussed her ongoing anxiety about recurrence, which is entirely understandable and appropriate given her history. She was counseled regarding signs and symptoms of recurrence (new lumps, unexpected weight loss, abdominal discomfort, neurological symptoms, skin changes) for which she should contact our office promptly between scheduled visits. She was referred to our cancer survivorship program and encouraged to continue attending her existing support group. She expressed gratitude for her care and remains engaged and motivated.

Return to clinic in approximately 3 months for first post-treatment surveillance labs and imaging review. Next CT scan to be coordinated through our scheduling team. Contact information for the oncology nurse triage line provided and reviewed.

   Alteration Type: Missense mutation, single nucleotide variant

FDA-approved combination targeted therapies available: dabrafenib (Tafinlar) + trametinib (Mekinist), vemurafenib (Zelboraf) + cobimetinib (Cotellic), and encorafenib (Braftovi) + binimetinib (Mektovi).    Classification: Pathogenic; loss of tumor suppressor
   Clinical Significance: CDKN2A encodes both p16/INK4A (a CDK4/6 inhibitor that suppresses cell cycle entry via Rb pathway) and p14/ARF (a positive regulator of p53 via MDM2). ========================================================
BIOMARKER SUMMARY
========================================================

Microsatellite Instability (MSI) Status:
  Result: MICROSATELLITE STABLE (MSS)
  Method: Fragment analysis of 5 mononucleotide repeat markers (BAT-25, BAT-26, MONO-27, NR-21, NR-24)
  Interpretation: No MSI-H detected. MSI-H is uncommon in cutaneous melanoma (<5% of cases). MSS status is consistent with expected biology for this tumor type.

Tumor Mutational Burden (TMB):
  Result: 42 mutations/Mb — HIGH
  Method: Calculated from somatic nonsynonymous SNVs and indels across the coding regions of the 500-gene panel; germline variants subtracted using validated filtering algorithm.
  Interpretation: TMB of 42 mut/Mb is markedly elevated and places this tumor in the high TMB category (threshold: ≥10 mut/Mb per FDA pembrolizumab tumor-agnostic approval). This is consistent with the UV-driven mutational landscape of cutaneous melanoma, where median TMB is typically 10–20 mut/Mb; the exceptionally high TMB in this case may reflect extensive UV damage accumulated over decades in the context of dysplastic nevus syndrome. High TMB is associated with increased neoantigen burden and, in many tumor types, improved response to immune checkpoint inhibition. Note: this assay-based TMB result is consistent with the overall high mutational complexity observed and is reinforced by PD-L1 TPS 30% / CPS 55 by IHC.

Mutational Signature Analysis:
  Dominant Signature: UV-induced mutagenesis (COSMIC Signature SBS7a/SBS7b)
  Interpretation: The predominant mutational signature is consistent with ultraviolet radiation-induced C>T transitions at dipyrimidine contexts (CC>TT dinucleotide substitutions), the hallmark mutational pattern of solar radiation-driven skin cancers. This finding is strongly concordant with the clinical diagnosis of cutaneous melanoma arising on sun-exposed trunk skin in a patient with longstanding dysplastic nevus syndrome. No significant tobacco signature (SBS4), APOBEC signature (SBS2/SBS13), or homologous recombination deficiency signature (SBS3) detected.

HER2 (ERBB2) Status: No amplification, no activating mutation detected.

========================================================
GERMLINE DISCLAIMER
========================================================

This assay is performed on tumor (somatic) tissue and is NOT a germline genetic test. Variants detected may be somatic, germline, or clonal hematopoietic in origin. Variants with potential germline relevance (e.g., TP53 p.R248W at VAF 41%) may warrant referral to a certified genetic counselor for germline evaluation in the appropriate clinical context, particularly given the patient's personal history of dysplastic nevus syndrome and family history of cutaneous melanoma in a first-degree relative. Germline evaluation is outside the scope of this report.

Note regarding TP53 p.R248W (VAF 41%): The elevated VAF for this variant, in the context of estimated 65% tumor cellularity, is compatible with a somatic heterozygous mutation. However, given the strong family history and personal history, germline TP53 testing may be considered at the discretion of the treating team and genetic counseling.

========================================================
INTERPRETATION AND MOLECULAR PROFILE SUMMARY
========================================================

This comprehensive NGS profile reveals a complex genomic landscape consistent with aggressive Stage IV UV-induced cutaneous melanoma. The dominant molecular subtype is BRAF V600E-driven (MAPK pathway-activated), with co-occurring alterations that elaborate on the tumor's biology and potential therapeutic vulnerabilities:

1. BRAF V600E (VAF 48%) — Confirmed oncogenic driver; directly actionable with FDA-approved BRAF/MEK inhibitor combinations (dabrafenib/trametinib, vemurafenib/cobimetinib, or encorafenib/binimetinib).

2. NF1 p.Q1300* (VAF 52%) — RAS pathway activator via loss of neurofibromin GAP function. Co-occurrence with BRAF V600E is notable; NF1 loss may drive RAS-mediated bypass signaling and contribute to early acquired resistance to BRAF inhibition.

3. CDKN2A/CDKN2B homozygous co-deletion — Complete loss of INK4 locus tumor suppressors (p16, p15, and p14/ARF), eliminating CDK4/6 inhibitory checkpoints and p53 stabilization via ARF-MDM2 axis.

4. High TMB (42 mut/Mb) — Markedly elevated, consistent with UV mutagenesis; favorable biomarker for immune checkpoint inhibitor responsiveness.

5. PD-L1 TPS 30% / CPS 55 (per concurrent IHC) — Consistent with potential immunogenicity of this tumor.

6. PTEN p.L108R (VAF 22%) — Subclonal PI3K/AKT pathway activation; potential resistance mechanism to BRAF-targeted therapy.

7. MAP2K1 copy number gain (5 copies) — Potential intrinsic mechanism of MEK pathway amplification; may reduce efficacy of MEK inhibitor component of BRAF/MEK combination.

8. RAC1 p.P29S (VAF 18%) — UV-signature hotspot, potentially subclonal; associated with primary resistance to BRAF inhibitors through non-MAPK mechanisms.

9. TERT promoter C228T (VAF 55%) — Clonal hallmark mutation of cutaneous melanoma; confirms telomere maintenance as a fundamental feature of this tumor.

10. TP53 p.R248W (VAF 41%) and ARID2 frameshift — Additional tumor suppressor and chromatin remodeling disruption; contribute to overall genomic instability and aggressive phenotype.

OVERALL MOLECULAR INTERPRETATION: This tumor harbors a BRAF V600E-mutant melanoma with multiple co-occurring alterations (NF1 loss, PTEN mutation, MAP2K1 gain, RAC1 p.P29S) that collectively suggest potential for early or acquired resistance to BRAF/MEK-targeted therapy. The markedly elevated TMB and the tumor's PD-L1 expression profile additionally support strong candidacy for immune checkpoint inhibitor therapy, either as an initial strategy or following targeted therapy. Clinical trial enrollment should be considered.

========================================================
FDA-APPROVED MATCHED THERAPIES (THIS TUMOR TYPE)
========================================================

- Dabrafenib (Tafinlar) + Trametinib (Mekinist): FDA-approved for BRAF V600E/K-mutant unresectable or metastatic melanoma [BRAF V600E confirmed]
- Vemurafenib (Zelboraf) + Cobimetinib (Cotellic): FDA-approved for BRAF V600E/K-mutant unresectable or metastatic melanoma [BRAF V600E confirmed]
- Encorafenib (Braftovi) + Binimetinib (Mektovi): FDA-approved for BRAF V600E/K-mutant unresectable or metastatic melanoma [BRAF V600E confirmed]
- Pembrolizumab (Keytruda): FDA-approved for unresectable or metastatic melanoma; also FDA-approved for TMB-High (≥10 mut/Mb) solid tumors [TMB 42 mut/Mb]
- Nivolumab (Opdivo): FDA-approved for unresectable or metastatic melanoma (monotherapy and in combination with ipilimumab)
- Ipilimumab (Yervoy) + Nivolumab (Opdivo): FDA-approved for unresectable or metastatic melanoma
- Ipilimumab (Yervoy) monotherapy: FDA-approved for unresectable or metastatic melanoma
- Nivolumab (Opdivo) + Relatlimab (Opdualag): FDA-approved for unresectable or metastatic melanoma

========================================================
TECHNICAL NOTES AND LIMITATIONS
========================================================

- This assay detects somatic alterations with VAF ≥5% and does not detect alterations below this threshold. Low-level subclonal variants may be missed.
- Homopolymer regions and regions of low complexity may have reduced sensitivity for indel detection.
- Copy number analysis requires adequate tumor purity (≥20%); estimated tumor content of 65% is sufficient.
- This panel does not comprehensively assess all possible alterations in all cancer-relevant genes. Alterations in genes not included on the panel cannot be excluded.
- Variants of uncertain significance (VUS) not meeting reporting thresholds are not included in this report.
- Interpretation is based on current knowledge at the time of reporting; the clinical significance of certain variants may evolve as evidence accumulates.
- Tissue heterogeneity and clonal evolution may result in molecular differences between the specimen analyzed and other sites of metastatic disease.

ASSESSMENT

Ms. Osei-Bonsu is a 58-year-old never-smoking female with a longstanding background of dysplastic nevus syndrome and a family history of melanoma in a first-degree relative of her mother's generation (maternal uncle), who presents with a newly diagnosed Stage IV (AJCC 8th edition, M1c) cutaneous malignant melanoma arising from the back. The disease is characterized by bulky bilateral axillary lymphadenopathy (dominant left node 4.8 cm), multiple hepatic metastases (largest 3.2 cm, segments IV/VI/VII), and subcutaneous satellite nodules on the left flank. LDH is significantly elevated at 2.4x the upper limit of normal, consistent with M1c staging. There is no CNS involvement on brain MRI, which is an important favorable feature.

From a molecular standpoint, the tumor harbors a BRAF p.V600E activating mutation confirmed by both IHC and NGS (VAF 48%), which is the primary actionable driver and opens the door for BRAF-targeted therapy with a BRAF/MEK inhibitor combination. Additional co-occurring alterations include NF1 loss of function (which can contribute to RAS pathway activation as a potential resistance mechanism), CDKN2A homozygous deletion (a very common finding in melanoma), TP53 mutation, PTEN loss, TERT promoter mutation, and MAP2K1 amplification. The TMB is high at 42 mut/Mb, and PD-L1 TPS is 30% with a CPS of 55 - these features also make the tumor potentially responsive to immune checkpoint inhibition, and the combination of BRAF/MEK inhibition followed or combined with immunotherapy may ultimately be considered.

The clinical picture is one of symptomatic, rapidly progressive, bulky M1c melanoma with hepatic involvement and elevated LDH. This patient requires active and expedient systemic therapy. ECOG PS is 1. Organ function is adequate for therapy; transaminase elevation is felt to be related to hepatic metastases rather than intrinsic liver disease, and hepatitis serologies are negative.

Key decision point for initial systemic therapy: In BRAF V600E-mutant metastatic melanoma, both BRAF/MEK inhibitor combination targeted therapy and upfront dual checkpoint inhibitor immunotherapy (e.g., ipi/Opdivo) are established first-line options with survival benefit. For this patient, several factors favor initiating BRAF/MEK inhibition (specifically, Tafinlar/Mekinist) as first-line treatment:
1. Bulky, symptomatic hepatic disease requiring rapid response - targeted therapy typically achieves faster and deeper early response compared to immunotherapy in BRAF-mutant disease
2. Significantly elevated LDH (2.4x ULN) indicating high disease burden - a population where rapid initial disease control is especially important
3. Symptomatic disease (right upper quadrant pain, constitutional symptoms) and moderate/significant weight loss indicating a patient who benefits from prompt disease control

For these reasons, we will proceed with Tafinlar (Tafinlar) 150 mg orally twice daily in combination with Mekinist (Mekinist) 2 mg orally once daily as first-line systemic therapy. This regimen is well-established based on COMBI-d and COMBI-v trial data, with response rates of approximately 66-69% and improved survival compared to single-agent BRAF inhibition. The addition of tram serves to delay the development of acquired resistance through MEK bypass mechanisms and reduces the incidence of BRAF inhibitor-associated paradoxical cutaneous side effects (including keratoacanthomas and squamous cell carcinomas).

The patient has been extensively counseled regarding the risks, benefits, and alternatives to therapy, including the option of upfront immunotherapy, which remains a reasonable alternative. She understands that targeted therapy may provide a faster initial response but that acquired resistance commonly develops, typically within 6-12 months, at which point alternative strategies including immunotherapy would be pursued.
